speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 4.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we started out and we met a Jewish Russian soldier right there who gave us-- they seemed to have a lot of sugar. They gave us like a-- a sack of sugar. And to us, it was very good, because we were so hungry and so malnourished. It gave us a little of a pick-up. And they said that we should just keep on walking on the main road. And as we were walking, it was still-- all the dead soldiers, some of them on-- on horseback, dead, lying on the floor. They hadn't been picked up because it was right after-- [INAUDIBLE] And they said, when night falls, we should just knock on any door and they have to let us in to give us shelter, like the-- that's what this Russian soldier said. If not, we could just me-- see any Russian soldiers on the street, and they'll make sure that they take us in. And we did stop in one house. But of course, there were a lot of people there too. And they said, yes, we could stay in the stable but we can't stay in the house, because there are too many people already staying.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed there overnight. And we-- the morning, we still, you know, continued. And we arrived back to Bromberg, Bydogoszcz in Polish. And there, we were told, as we were asking, to go to the police station.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were given instructions how to get to the police station. And once there, we told them who we are and they instructed us, go, take this street or the other street, and they told us what streets, and go into any house. There's a lot of German houses. They are va-- vacated, that they left. Go into any German house and make yourself at home. And that's what we did. And there was a lot of food, which was-- but we were afraid to eat it, the prepared foods. They might be poisoned, so we didn't eat that. But there were some things in the basement-- some potatoes, some vegetables-- so we ate that. And we looked what we could take from there. And of course, I was so naive. There was a whole set of silverware. I needed one knife, so I took the one knife, because that's all I needed. And the rest, of course, some of the other girls took. I took some of the curtains off the window so we could make some clothes for ourselves from it. And we stayed in that house-- oh, about 20, 25 of us, whoever came, you know-- until we got word. Somebody came from the other end of town telling us our cousins are here, and they are there and could we go and stay with them. So we went and we stayed with them for a-- about two weeks. And one of my cousins got a nervous breakdown. Anyway, my one cousin and I, we were going around begging for food, because there was no way for us to get out of Bydogoszcz, or Bromberg. There was no trains running. There was nothing going yet. And there was no way for us to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after a few weeks, and after a few stealings and beggings, we managed to wait out on the first train left for Lublin. Because we were told in Lublin there is a Jewish community, and they have set up things to take care of these concentration camp survivors. And when did you get on that train?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in-- at the end of January. And in the meantime, we had a lot of problems with the Rus-- Russian soldiers because they wanted to rape every girl. And we had a l-- had a lot of, lot of problems. But we, thank God, made it. And a lot of problems on the train as well, because the train stopped in Warsaw. And there, a bunch of Russian soldiers-- drunk ones-- came onto the trains. But we managed to escape from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you and your sister stay away from them?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was hard. I mean, s-- it was things which I don't even want to describe what was done, you know, that-- to make them stay away from us and from the girls. And we got to Lublin, and there was a Jewish community who was waiting already at the train, because every day new ones would come. And they took us. It was set up in one of the shul, one of the synagogues. And there was a kitchen and they were feeding us and they were looking after us. And after about two weeks, there was some more trains leaving towards our home, through Czechoslovakia, Hungary, to Romania. But they-- those trains would stop and stay for days in places, and then go further and further. Some of them were freight trains. Some were passenger trains. Whatever we could get on. Now looking back, I don't know how I did it. It's unbelievable. But with my cousin being sick, having had the nervous breakdown, she didn't want to walk. She didn't want to eat. We used to force-feed her and pull her. Anyway, we got on to some of those trains and we made it. We stopped in towns. We got off. We'd get into houses where they would put us up for a day or two. Just any house, wherever we knocked on the door, they knew they had to do it, because they were told by the Russian army that they have to accommodate us. And then somehow, in Czechoslovakia already, there were passenger trains running. And we got-- I mean, no money or anything like that. We-- they wouldn't ask. We wouldn't ask for money. We wouldn't ask for tickets, and we didn't have any. And we got onto those trains and slowly made our way to Romania, to Satu Mare, where the train stopped. It was the last station. It was a train full of women. It was the first train full of concentration camp survivors coming back.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were a lot of Jewish men there already, who were in working camps-- in army working camps-- were freed already in November of 1944, when the Russians occupied Romania. And they were all waiting to hear from their wives, sisters, mothers, whoever. So there was a bunch of them waiting at the station and asking, did you hear from this one, did you hear or know or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, they took us. There was this Jewish-- to a hospital, which used to be the Jewish General Hospital at Satu Mare. And they put us up there for a couple of days. And then we went to stay with some of the survivors, with these people. And they took in-- you know, because they had-- because those men already had homes and had-- because they were there for home for months already, and they moved back into their homes. And we stayed there. Of course, it wasn't Satu Mare where I was sent off from, so we wanted to go and see what we could salvage from our town. In the meantime, my cousin and my-- and her sister were sent off to another mental hospital in Cluj, and my sister and I went home to Supuru de Jos. But we didn't find anything except a small picture of my mother. Nothing else. We came back, and we stayed with a family who I helped them and worked with them. And my sister stayed with me. She went to learn how to sew. And that New Year's Eve, we went-- I went to a New Year's party and I met my husband-to-be there. And we got engaged about in March. And got married in September, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you and your husband go?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we lived there. My husband had a-- he was one of those-- was in a working camp, so he already had a business. He had a china and a-- store, like china in-- and crystals and glassware, with a partner. And we got married and I lived-- we fixed up an apartment. And of course, we were by ourselves. And my sister came to live with us. And about at that time, we got a letter from Canada, which came through the joint, that they were looking for survivors. Because I-- my sister had a sister living-- and a brother living in Canada. And they were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your-- your hus--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's-- my mother's sister and brother were living in Canada, and they were looking for survivors. I didn't know their address. I couldn't look for them. And they-- and when we got that letter and they said that they want us in that letter, if-- if whoever is surviving, they want that we should let them know and they want us to come to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your husband have any surviving members of his family?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had one brother who was a prisoner in Russia. And he came back, but we had already left by then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband's name was Michael, [INAUDIBLE] and Smaye. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you leave for Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1947, in November. The place started to be very Communist there, you know? The Communists were coming to power and we were afraid to stay under Communist rule. So we decided to leave. We tried to get passports. We couldn't. They wouldn't let us out. And we, then, had somebody who lived at the border who used to come in to shop at my husband's place. And he paid them a-- a good sum of money and we gave them everything we had. And they took us to stay overnight there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it so happened that my husband's partner didn't know that we were leaving. Nobody, you know, whenever-- and ever anybody left, nobody-- they didn't tell anybody, because they were afraid they would be caught at the border. Somebody will tell. So my sister and I put on countless amount of dresses-- about seven, eight dresses-- what we could, and we had made ourselves some high boots, you know. And we-- this woman came in, and by horse and buggy she took us out to her place. And we stayed there all day in bed. She did-- she was afraid somebody shouldn't walk in or see us through the window. It was a farmhouse.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that night, she went out to get my husband. So my husband, you know, closed the store just like any other day. He just took off with her. And then, when he arrived around midnight, she had a way how to go across. She sort of knew when the-- when the soldiers sort of-- you know, from Romania to Hungary. When they-- you know, because they went back and forth on the border, there was a big ditch and a lot of mud. And she took us across to the first station across to Hungary. And there, we were so full of mud. We tried to clean ourself up, because we were-- we had to go on the train. And sh-- we had arranged everything with her that she would get our tickets. But when we came to the station, the station master said no, he wants more money. Told him we don't have. I had to take my ring off. I had to give it to him. Anyway, we were allowed onto the train and we came to Budapest. And we tried-- and we were told, don't stay together because you'll be suspicious. We-- we stood, you know, in different sections of the train, different trains. And we got to Budapest, where my husband had a cousin. And we stayed with them for a few weeks before we got somebody to smuggle us across to Austria, again without papers. Once we were smuggled across to Austria, Vienna was still divided into four-- French and English and American and Russian zones. And we had to-- we-- we were in the American zone as we c-- arrived, you know. And there was a Jewish joint distribution. And we stayed in Austria until we were able to buy ourselves papers and passports to go to Linz, Austria, which was already in-- we had to go through the Russian zone to get there, which where there were DP camps. And I remember getting a passport which didn't look like me at all, the picture. I didn't know the name. I had to study the name. But when, you see, the Russians used to come up on the train and check everybody's papers, you know, before they would let them go on to the other zone. And I pa-- we passed. And we get to Austria. To Linz, I mean. And there were two DP camps, Ebelsberg and Bindermichl, where were-- And then my sister came out to Canada with a children's transport, because she was so young. So they took children to Canada. And she arrived here. With my aunt's and uncle's help, she made papers for us. And six months later, we arrived to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in the DP camps for--

speaker: interviewee
text: For a year and a half. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then when we arrived here, we stayed with my aunt for a while. Then my husband was in work and then was in business. And we moved to Pembroke, Ontario, where he had a business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you start having a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1952. I arrived to Canada in 1949, in April, to Halifax, by boat from Napoli. And I was so sick. I thought, I'm not-- I will not make it. My husband-- of course, I was married by then-- he used to-- he was well. He used to carry me up on the deck every morning, carry me down every night. And we arrived. And then in 1952, my daughter was born in Montreal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ruth. Ruth Smaye Warner. Her married name is Warner. And then we moved-- she was a little over a year when we moved to Pembroke. And in Pembroke, my two other children were born, Sheldon David in 1955, and Audrey in 1958, December, like Sheldon was born in December. And in 1967, we moved to Toronto because, although there was a synagogue in Pembroke and we did have a teacher coming from Ottawa at first to teach the children Hebrew, and then we had a teacher living in-- there, teaching the children. But for my husband, he felt it wasn't enough. He wanted the children to have more Jewish education. And we moved, but he stayed on in Pembroke. He used to just commute every weekend, come back, for another three years, about. And then he sold the business and we lived here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you still religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my children too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you come forward to tell your story now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came forward because, as my children were growing up, I never talked about my experiences. I didn't want to sadden them. Although the children, as they were growing up, they always asked the question, how come we don't have a grandmother, grandfather, aunts, uncles, lots of cousins, like all their friends. Because especially in Pembroke, they grew up and they-- of course, everybody had. And we told them some, but we never told them the story, what happened. And now I just feel that-- that my children and grandchildren should know [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what went on in those years. And what I went through [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and what the ones who didn't make it went through, [SOBBING] which is even worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course, there were times that I thought maybe it's best for them who didn't make it, because why-- why was I spared? And I was thinking like that very, very often. And to me, once the children started coming, it was the most precious thing, because that's all I had. [CRYING] But, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I really didn't want to sadden them. And now I would like them to know and see. And especially my grandchildren, because by that time, when they will be growing up, there will be very few survivors, and they might not even hear the stories, you know, accurately. And of course, this was a very-- I just came through it-- I mean, but to live it. And there were so many other details which I would prob-- probably need a day of taping to go through that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a closing statement you'd like to make?

speaker: interviewee
text: All I would like to that whoever hears or sees these videos should think and know and especially what-- what people could do to people. And this should never, never, never happen again, what happened in those years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Smaye, thank you very much for telling me your story. Now we'll take a look at some photographs, and then we'll meet some of your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture, that was given to me by my aunt who was in Canada since before the war-- she came in 1938 to Canada. And this picture was enlarged when she brought it out with her. Now, this is from my mother's side, my maternal grandparents, and some of her sisters and brothers. They were nine in the family. And here are six sisters and their spouses, and three brothers. Now, starting from the left, which you could only see part of him, his name is Paisah Hersch. And my mother's sister-in-law, her name is Rifchu. Her brother is Shapse. And the next one is Sam Taback, who was a survivor because he was here since before the war, living in Montreal. Next to him is my uncle [? Ber ?] Moishe, who is uncle, like, from-- like a brother-in-law to my mother. The next one is Motti, another brother-in-law, and Moishe, another brother-in-law. And the next is Ruchama, a sister of my mother. And down from right to left, is Sasa, a sister; Leah, a sister. And the picture they are holding is the husband of my aunt at the time when she was on her way to Canada. And he had been here already. And his name is Arthur Barch, who is still alive, thank God. And my aunt is alive, but my uncle who lived in Montreal passed away. My grandparents, Yosef Taback and Sheindl Taback, they passed away just-- and-- just before the war. And those are my three cousins, from left to right, Sholem, Ete, and Motti. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] That's a picture of my mother, as I was saying before. When they were carting out our stuff, our furniture and things, it must have fallen out. Because that's the only thing that I got back from a neighbor who found it on the ground. Nothing else. And I have no other mementos from my parents, pictures of my brothers or sisters. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] That's my wedding pictures-- picture. I was married in Satu Mare, Romania, 19-- September 10th, 1946. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I met my husband about nine months before that, New Year's Eve, 1945. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] His name is M-- [INAUDIBLE] Smaye was there in Romania. But he-- we changed the name and it-- we made it to Smaye. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] That's a picture taken in Pembroke, Ontario in 1959. My hus-- from left, my husband Mike, my youngest daughter Audrey, my son Sheldon, my daughter Ruth, and myself. Aud-- Audrey was about nine months old at the time. And it's an old picture, but it's very precious to me. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture was taken in December, 1994. These are all my grandchildren. They were all here for Hanukkah, and it was insisted that I have a picture taken with them. And from left to right, it's Michael Smaye, and next is Hadassa Male, myself, and then Yon-- Jonathan Warner. The next one is Batcheva Mae and Jachil-- Jachil Male. And my granddaughter Rochama Warner. And left to right again, it's Eta Male, Michael Warner, and Amil Warner. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This card was given to us in the DP camps in Austria when we arrived in 1948, just to be as a-- a concentration camp survivor. We were in the DP camp in Ebelsberg and Bindermichl, Linz, Austria. And with this card, we were able to move around in the city, as an identification. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This document lists the concentration camps that I wa-- was at. But not all of them, because some of them were really not concentration camps, just working camps. And the numbers-- the two different numbers I had. One was given to me in Riga, and the other one in Stutthof. And this picture and document was given to me in 1948 in Austria. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is my oldest daughter, Ruth Smaye Warner. These are her children-- my grandchildren-- Rochama [? Margolit ?] Warner, Michael Warner, and Amil Warner, and Jonathan Warner. These are her children. My other grandchildren are not here. They are in Chicago and another one in another school here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
